title: Nautical Terms
description: This topic covers the essential vocabulary of a sailing yacht: hull geometry, structural parts above and below deck, the standing and running rigging, points of sailing relative to the wind, and the main rig types encountered on cruising and racing yachts.
summary: This topic covers the essential vocabulary of a sailing yacht: hull geometry, structural parts above and below deck, the standing and running rigging, points of sailing relative to the wind, and the main rig types encountered on cruising and racing yachts. Proficiency with these terms is a prerequisite for every other aspect of the Day Skipper syllabus.

keyTerms.definition: Forward end of the vessel
Aft (rear) end of the vessel
Left side when facing forward
Right side when facing forward
Widest point of the hull; also the athwartships direction
Middle of the vessel, fore-and-aft or athwartships
Height of hull side above the waterline
Depth of hull below the waterline
Underwater fin providing ballast and lateral resistance
Flat or curved panel forming the stern
Hull sides between waterline and deck
Main entrance hatch from cockpit to below deck
Wire from masthead to bow, supports mast forward
Side wires supporting the mast athwartships
Horizontal spar attached to mast at gooseneck
Rope used to hoist a sail
Rope used to control a sail's angle to the wind
Leading edge of a sail
Trailing edge of a sail
Point of sail closest to the wind (~45°)
Sailing with wind at 90° to the centreline
Sailing with wind from behind and to the side
Sailing with wind directly from astern
Turning bow through the wind to change tacks
Turning stern through the wind to change tacks
Boat is head to wind and stalled in no-go zone
Fore-and-aft wire/webbing on deck for safety tether clip-on
Space below the cabin sole
Onboard toilet compartment
Pedestal housing the steering compass
Mooring or anchor rope
Mooring line from the bow to a point ashore
Mooring line from the stern to a point ashore
Diagonal mooring line that prevents the vessel moving ahead or astern
Short line holding a vessel directly alongside another vessel in a raft
Deck fitting that guides a rope in the correct direction
Inflatable or foam buffer hung over the side to protect the hull
Shore-side post to which mooring lines are secured
Two or more boats moored alongside each other
Berth between two piles with the bow or stern to a pontoon
Short pontoon projecting from the main pontoon alongside which one boat moors
Line hanging from a pile to assist retrieval of the mooring line

flashcards.front: What is the term for the left side of the vessel when facing forward?
flashcards.back: Port

flashcards.front: What is the no-go zone and approximately how wide is it?
flashcards.back: The arc directly into the wind where sails cannot fill; approximately 45° either side of the wind direction

flashcards.front: What does "draught" mean?
flashcards.back: The depth of the keel below the waterline — the minimum water depth needed to float the vessel

flashcards.front: Name the three edges of a sail
flashcards.back: Luff (leading edge), leech (trailing edge), foot (bottom edge)

flashcards.front: What is the difference between tacking and gybing?
flashcards.back: Tacking turns the bow through the wind; gybing turns the stern through the wind

flashcards.front: What is a jackstay?
flashcards.back: A strong fore-and-aft wire or webbing on deck used to attach safety tethers

flashcards.front: What is the function of the kicking strap (vang)?
flashcards.back: Prevents the boom from rising and controls sail shape, particularly when running

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a fin keel and a long keel?
flashcards.back: A fin keel is short and deep (modern cruising); a long keel runs most of the hull length (traditional)

flashcards.front: How long should mooring warps be when securing alongside a tidal wall?
flashcards.back: At least 4× the rise and fall of tide

flashcards.front: When mooring in a raft, which boat should take its own lines ashore?
flashcards.back: The outside boat — to minimise strain on the inside boat's shore lines

flashcards.front: Why do pontoon mooring lines not need to be adjusted as the tide rises and falls?
flashcards.back: Because the floating pontoon rises and falls with the tide

flashcards.front: What is a spring line used for?
flashcards.back: A diagonal mooring line that prevents the vessel from moving ahead (bow spring) or astern (stern spring)

mcqQuestions.question: Q: Which point of sail has the wind coming directly from abeam (90° to the centreline)?
What is the maximum angle a boat can generally sail relative to the true wind without the sails collapsing?
What term describes the depth of a vessel's keel below the waterline?
The forestay supports the mast in which direction?
What is the term for the fitting at the base of the mast where the boom is attached?
On which tack does the wind come over the port side?
What is the bilge?
options: The interior ceiling of the yacht
The space below the cabin sole

mcqQuestions.question: When mooring alongside a tidal wall, what is the minimum recommended length for mooring warps?
options: 2× the rise and fall of tide
3× the rise and fall of tide
4× the rise and fall of tide
6× the rise and fall of tide

mcqQuestions.question: When moored in a raft, the outside boat should:
options: Rely entirely on breast ropes to the inside boat
Take its own bow and stern lines ashore to minimise strain on the inside boat
Use a single long warp running from bow to stern
Attach lines only to the inside boat, not ashore

mcqQuestions.question: What is the purpose of a bow spring (forespring)?
options: To hold the bow close to the dock
To prevent the vessel from moving astern
To prevent the vessel from moving forward
To stop the vessel heeling at the berth

title: Ropework
description: This topic covers the properties and construction of synthetic ropes used aboard sailing yachts, the correct techniques for securing ropes to cleats and winches, and the principal knots used in sailing with their specific applications.
summary: This topic covers the properties and construction of synthetic ropes used aboard sailing yachts, the correct techniques for securing ropes to cleats and winches, and the principal knots used in sailing with their specific applications. Understanding which knot to use in which situation is a practical safety skill as well as a knowledge-test topic.

keyTerms.definition: Low-stretch synthetic rope material; standard for sheets and halyards
Stretchy synthetic rope; absorbs shock loads — used for warps and anchor rodes
Lightweight, floating synthetic rope; limited UV resistance
Three-strand twisted rope construction
Rope with woven outer sheath and inner core
Knot forming a fixed, secure loop at the end of a rope

keyTerms.term: Round turn and two half hitches
keyTerms.definition: Hitch to a ring/rail; can be released under load
Flat knot for joining two ends of the same rope (e.g. reef points)
Quick-to-tie hitch; used for securing fenders
Non-slip hitch used to take the strain off a loaded rope or spar
Stopper knot preventing a rope end running through a block
Knot for joining two ropes, especially of different diameters
Automatic gripping device on a winch drum
A dangerous crossed turn on a winch drum, difficult to release under load
Mechanical device that holds a rope under load, like a cleat

flashcards.front: Which rope material is best for mooring lines and anchor warps, and why?
flashcards.back: Nylon — its elasticity absorbs shock loads

flashcards.front: What knot is used to make a fixed, non-slipping loop in a rope?
flashcards.back: Bowline

flashcards.front: Which knot should be used to secure a fender to a guard rail?
flashcards.back: Clove hitch — quick to tie and easy to adjust

flashcards.front: What is a rolling hitch used for?
flashcards.back: Taking the strain off a loaded rope or spar when the pull is at an angle

flashcards.front: Why is a reef knot not suitable for joining two ropes of different diameters?
flashcards.back: It is not secure when used for two ropes of different sizes or under varying load; it can capsize

flashcards.front: What must you do before releasing a rope under load from a jammer?
flashcards.back: Take the strain on a winch first — never hold the rope close to the jammer

flashcards.front: In which direction must rope be wound around a winch drum?
flashcards.back: Clockwise

mcqQuestions.question: Q: Which rope material floats and is therefore useful for throw lines?
Which knot creates a fixed eye in a rope and remains secure under tension?
A rolling hitch is most useful when:
options: Joining two ropes of different diameters
Making fast to a cleat quickly
Taking the strain off a loaded rope pulled at an angle
Stopping a rope from running through a block

mcqQuestions.question: What is a riding turn on a winch, and how is it best prevented?
options: A figure-of-eight wrap; prevented by using a jammer
A crossed turn that jams under load; prevented by keeping the rope lead low
A turn used to secure the selftailer; prevented by using nylon rope
An extra half hitch; prevented by winding anticlockwise

mcqQuestions.question: Which knot is used to join two ropes, particularly ropes of different diameters?

title: Anchorwork
description: This topic covers the main anchor types used on cruising yachts, the principles of scope calculation for chain and warp, how to select a suitable anchorage, and the practical considerations for anchoring safely including swinging room, seabed type, and tidal awareness.
summary: This topic covers the main anchor types used on cruising yachts, the principles of scope calculation for chain and warp, how to select a suitable anchorage, and the practical considerations for anchoring safely including swinging room, seabed type, and tidal awareness. Correct anchoring technique is important both for safety and for environmental reasons.

keyTerms.definition: Length of anchor rode (chain or warp) paid out
The chain or warp connecting the anchor to the vessel
Rope used as an anchor rode (as opposed to chain)
Metal link cable used as anchor rode; heavy, helps hold angle low
Three-claw anchor with good holding power; difficult to stow
Rigid plough-type anchor suitable for bow roller; self-launches
Twin-fluke flat anchor; stows easily; can foul in mud
Hinged plough-type anchor; can capsize due to moving parts
Traditional stock anchor; suits rocky/weedy seabeds
Clear water radius needed for the vessel to swing around its anchor
The quality of the seabed for anchor holding (sand/mud good; rock/shingle poor)
Recovering (weighing) an anchor from the seabed

flashcards.front: What scope is recommended for a chain anchor rode?
flashcards.back: Minimum 4 × the maximum depth of water during the stay

flashcards.front: What scope is recommended for a warp anchor rode?
flashcards.back: Minimum 6 × the maximum depth of water during the stay

flashcards.front: Which anchor type is best suited to rocky or weedy seabeds?
flashcards.back: Fisherman's anchor

flashcards.front: Why is the Danforth anchor sometimes difficult to recover?
flashcards.back: It can be hard to break out of mud

flashcards.front: What two seabed types give the best holding for most anchors?
flashcards.back: Mud and sand

flashcards.front: Why must maximum tidal depth (not current depth) be used for scope calculation?
flashcards.back: The depth increases with the rising tide; insufficient scope at high water could cause the anchor to drag

flashcards.front: Why should you not anchor on a leading line?
flashcards.back: Other vessels may be using it to navigate into harbour and could run you down

mcqQuestions.question: Q: A boat anchors in 3 metres of water using chain. What is the minimum scope recommended?
A boat anchors in 4 metres using warp. What minimum length of warp should be paid out?
Which anchor is designed to remain on the bow roller for self-launching and has no moving parts?
Which type of holding ground gives the poorest anchor hold for most modern anchors?
When anchoring in a mixed anchorage, why might boats of different types collide even when none drag their anchors?
options: Boats with high windage swing differently from deep-draught, low-windage boats
Chain rodes are shorter than warp rodes
All boats face the same direction in all conditions

title: Safety
description: Safety is a foundational topic in the RYA Day Skipper syllabus.
summary: Safety is a foundational topic in the RYA Day Skipper syllabus. It covers the equipment required on board a pleasure vessel, personal protective gear for the crew, fire prevention and fighting, and what to do in an emergency.

keyTerms.definition: Personal buoyancy device designed to turn an unconscious person face-up in the water and keep the airway clear. Usually gas-inflation type.
Lower-buoyancy device (typically foam) that assists a conscious swimmer but does not turn an unconscious person face-up. Suitable for inshore/dinghy sailing.
Webbing worn over the body with strong attachment points; connected by a tether (harness line) to a jackstay or strong point to prevent a crew member going overboard.
A wire or webbing line rigged along the deck (port and starboard) to which harness tethers are clipped, allowing crew to move safely on deck in heavy weather.

Emergency Position-Indicating Radio Beacon. A vessel-mounted device that, when activated (automatically on immersion or manually), transmits a distress signal with GPS position via satellite to COSPAS-SARSAT.
Personal Locator Beacon. A smaller, personal version of an EPIRB, carried by an individual crew member.
An inflatable survival raft, stored in a canister on deck, deployed if the vessel must be abandoned. Equipped with a canopy, drogue, and survival equipment.
The line connecting the liferaft canister to the vessel; when pulled, it triggers the inflation mechanism.
A sea anchor or drag device streamed from the liferaft to slow drift and reduce the risk of capsizing.
Aqueous Film-Forming Foam — fire extinguisher medium effective against flammable liquid fires (Class B).
Fire extinguisher that smothers flames by displacing oxygen; effective in enclosed spaces such as the engine bay; leaves no residue.
International voice distress signal (from French "m'aidez" — help me) indicating grave and imminent danger to life or vessel. Transmitted on VHF Ch 16.
International urgency signal indicating a serious situation requiring assistance but not immediate danger to life.
Digital Selective Calling — a feature of modern VHF radios allowing a digital distress alert (including MMSI and, if GPS-linked, position) to be sent at the press of a button.
Maritime Mobile Service Identity — a unique 9-digit number assigned to a DSC-equipped vessel; included in DSC distress calls.
A rocket-launched red flare that deploys a parachute, burning for approximately 40 seconds at altitude for maximum range and visibility; long-range distress signal.
A handheld red flare used to pinpoint the vessel's position at short range, inshore or when rescuers are already in sight.
Daytime distress/position signal — canister (floating or handheld) producing dense orange smoke visible from air and sea.
A through-hull fitting below the waterline; if it fails, the vessel will flood. Should be adjacent to a softwood bung of matching size.

safetyNotes: NEVER fire a parachute flare if a helicopter is approaching — it could strike the aircraft and cause a catastrophe.
NEVER fire a flare into the wind.
Do NOT attach a helicopter's weighted guide line to any part of the vessel.
Do NOT open the engine compartment to fight a fire — this introduces oxygen. Use an automatic or remotely activated extinguisher.
A lifejacket must be worn, not just carried. In an emergency there may be no time to don it.
Abandonment should be a last resort: the vessel is usually easier for rescuers to find than a liferaft. Only abandon ship when the vessel is clearly going to sink or is on fire and uncontrollable.
Gas (LPG) is heavier than air — it accumulates unseen in the bilges. Always ventilate before starting the engine or using naked flames below decks.
A mobile phone CANNOT replace VHF for distress communications — it alerts only one person and cannot be used for two-way communication with a helicopter.

flashcards.front: When should a harness be worn on deck?
flashcards.back: In bad weather and when going forward on deck at night.

flashcards.front: What is the correct sequence for a Mayday voice call?
flashcards.back: "Mayday, Mayday, Mayday — This is [vessel name] × 3 — Mayday [vessel name] — position — nature of distress — persons on board — Over."

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a Mayday and a Pan Pan call?
flashcards.back: Mayday = grave and imminent danger to life or vessel. Pan Pan = urgent situation requiring assistance, but not immediately life-threatening.

flashcards.front: What type of fire extinguisher is best for an engine space?
flashcards.back: CO₂ or halocarbon — effective in enclosed spaces; ideally automatic or remotely activated so the compartment is not opened and oxygen is not introduced.

flashcards.front: Why is VHF better than a mobile phone in a distress situation?
flashcards.back: VHF alerts all vessels within range, can be used by coastguard to take a direction-finding fix, and allows direct communication with lifeboat and helicopter.

flashcards.front: How do you clear petrol/LPG vapour from below decks?
flashcards.back: Open hatches and turn downwind to vent fresh air through the boat. Do NOT use the bilge pump — it will not clear gas effectively.

flashcards.front: When abandoning to a liferaft, which crew member should board first?
flashcards.back: The heaviest and strongest, to stabilise the raft and assist others in boarding.

flashcards.front: What course should the helm steer during a helicopter rescue?
flashcards.back: A very steady course, typically with the wind 30° on the port bow.

flashcards.front: What are the three types of fire extinguisher described for use on yachts, and what is each used for?
flashcards.back: (1) Dry powder — general use, not for flammable liquids. (2) CO₂/halocarbon — enclosed spaces/engine. (3) AFFF foam — flammable liquids.

flashcards.front: What distress signal should NEVER be fired if a helicopter is approaching?
flashcards.back: A parachute (rocket) flare — it could strike the aircraft.

mcqQuestions.question: Q: Which distress signal is used when life or vessel are in grave and imminent danger?
What is the advantage of a DSC-equipped VHF radio in an emergency?
options: It operates on a different frequency to avoid interference
It can send a digital distress alert including vessel position at the press of a button
It gives longer range than a standard VHF
It is linked directly to the RNLI

mcqQuestions.question: Where should fire extinguishers be positioned on a yacht?
options: All stored in one accessible location in the cockpit
Near the exit to each accommodation space; engine space to have its own automatic or remotely activated unit
Stored below the chart table for security

mcqQuestions.question: When should the engine space be vented before starting an inboard petrol engine?
options: Only if the bilge alarm has sounded
Always — to clear any accumulated petrol vapour before ignition
Never — ventilation is not required for petrol engines

mcqQuestions.question: Which flare type provides the longest-range distress signal and is most suitable for offshore use at night?
What should you do immediately if you find you cannot control a fire on board?
options: Continue fighting — fires always burn out
Get everyone on deck with lifejackets and be prepared to abandon ship
Start the engine to reach harbour quickly

mcqQuestions.question: Which of the following is the correct action when a weighted line is lowered from a rescue helicopter?
options: Attach it to the mast to stabilise the helicopter's position
Gather in the slack but do not attach it to the boat
Attach it to the anchor chain
Pull it towards the boat to guide the rescue swimmer

title: IRPCS/COLREGS: International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea
description: The International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea (IRPCS) — commonly called COLREGS — are a set of internationally agreed rules that govern how vessels must behave to avoid collisions.
summary: The International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea (IRPCS) — commonly called COLREGS — are a set of internationally agreed rules that govern how vessels must behave to avoid collisions. They are given force in UK law by the Merchant Shipping (Distress Signals and Prevention of Collisions) Regulations 1996 (MSN 1781 M+F).

keyTerms.definition: International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea — the internationally agreed collision avoidance rules. Also called COLREGS.
Common abbreviation for the Convention on the International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972. Synonymous with IRPCS in practical use.
The vessel required by the rules to take avoiding action to keep clear of another.
The vessel required to maintain its course and speed while the give-way vessel takes action.
Two power-driven vessels crossing so as to involve risk of collision — the vessel that has the other on her starboard side is the give-way vessel.
Two power-driven vessels meeting on reciprocal (or near-reciprocal) courses — both must alter course to starboard.
A vessel coming up on another from a direction more than 22.5° abaft the beam is overtaking and must keep clear.
A defined navigable waterway where large vessels can only safely navigate within the channel. Specific rules apply (Rule 9).
An IMO-approved routing measure that separates opposing streams of vessel traffic in heavily used sea lanes. Rule 10 applies.
A black shape (ball, cone, cylinder, diamond, etc.) hoisted by day to indicate a vessel's type or activity. The daytime equivalent of navigation lights.
A white masthead light visible over an arc of 225°, carried by power-driven vessels underway.

A single combined red/green/white navigation light fitted at the masthead of a sailing vessel under 20 m; used as an alternative to separate sidelights and sternlight. Must NOT be used simultaneously with a steaming light.
A black cone, apex down, hoisted by a sailing vessel when also using her engine — the vessel is then treated as power-driven.

Exists if the compass bearing to another vessel does not appreciably change — assessed by taking a series of bearings or by observing whether another vessel remains lined up against a fixed point on your own vessel.

safetyNotes: The IRPCS apply to ALL vessels at all times on all waters navigable by seagoing vessels. Ignorance of the rules is not a defence.
Being the "stand-on" vessel does not mean doing nothing — if the give-way vessel fails to act, the stand-on vessel must ultimately manoeuvre to avoid collision.
Give-way action must be early and bold — a small, late alteration of course may be misread by the other vessel and make the situation worse.
In a narrow channel, do not assume right of way over large vessels — they may have no room to manoeuvre and a collision would be catastrophic.
A vessel overtaking another remains the give-way vessel until well past and clear — even if the other vessel appears to slow down.
Sound signals are mandatory — five short blasts means danger or doubt; if you hear it, something is wrong.

flashcards.front: How do you assess whether a risk of collision exists?
flashcards.back: Take compass bearings of the approaching vessel. If the bearing does not change (or the vessel stays lined up with a fixed reference on your boat), a risk of collision exists.

flashcards.front: In a crossing situation between two power vessels, which gives way?
flashcards.back: The vessel that has the other on her starboard side gives way.

flashcards.front: What must both vessels do in a head-on situation?
flashcards.back: Both must alter course to starboard.

flashcards.front: In the order of priority, which vessel type has the highest right of way?
flashcards.back: A vessel restricted in ability to manoeuvre (e.g. laying marks, dredging).

flashcards.front: What does a sailing vessel display by day when also motoring?
flashcards.back: A black cone, apex downwards (the motoring cone).

flashcards.front: What does the term "stand-on vessel" mean?
flashcards.back: The vessel required to maintain its course and speed while the give-way vessel takes avoiding action.

flashcards.front: In a narrow channel, does power automatically give way to sail?
flashcards.back: No — in a narrow channel, a vessel constrained to remain in the channel must not be impeded by small craft that can safely navigate outside it.

flashcards.front: What is the five-short-blasts sound signal?
flashcards.back: The danger/doubt signal — indicating that another vessel's intentions are unclear or that it is not taking sufficient action to avoid collision.

mcqQuestions.question: In a crossing situation between two power-driven vessels, which vessel is the give-way vessel?
options: The vessel that has the other on her port side
The vessel that has the other on her starboard side

mcqQuestions.question: What is the correct action for both vessels in a power-to-power head-on situation?
options: Both vessels slow down and wait
Both vessels alter course to port
Both vessels alter course to starboard
The vessel on the right gives way

mcqQuestions.question: Which of the following vessel types has the highest priority in the IRPCS order of precedence?
options: Vessel restricted in ability to manoeuvre

mcqQuestions.question: A sailing vessel of 15 m is motoring. What must she display by day?
options: Nothing — day shapes are only required for vessels over 20 m

mcqQuestions.question: In a narrow channel, when may a small sailing vessel be given right of way over a large power-driven vessel?
options: Always — sail has priority over power
Only if the sailing vessel is overtaking
Never — in a narrow channel, vessels constrained to the channel must not be impeded

title: Position, Course and Speed
description: This topic defines the fundamental units and concepts of marine navigation: how position is expressed using latitude and longitude, how the nautical mile and the knot relate to the Earth's geometry, and the four types of bearing (true, magnetic, compass, and relative).
summary: This topic defines the fundamental units and concepts of marine navigation: how position is expressed using latitude and longitude, how the nautical mile and the knot relate to the Earth's geometry, and the four types of bearing (true, magnetic, compass, and relative). These definitions underpin all chartwork and passage planning.

keyTerms.definition: Angular distance north or south of the Equator, measured in degrees and minutes
Angular distance east or west of the Greenwich Meridian, measured in degrees and minutes
Unit of distance at sea = 1 minute of latitude = 1852 m
Unit of speed = 1 nautical mile per hour
Bearing measured clockwise from True North
Bearing measured from Magnetic North
Reading from the vessel's compass (includes deviation)
Bearing measured from the vessel's bow
Difference between True North and Magnetic North at a given location
Error in compass reading caused by the vessel's own magnetic field
Line of longitude — runs pole to pole
Line of latitude — runs parallel to the Equator

flashcards.front: How many metres are in one nautical mile?
flashcards.back: 1852 metres

flashcards.front: What is a knot?
flashcards.back: One nautical mile per hour

flashcards.front: One minute of latitude equals what distance?
flashcards.back: One nautical mile

flashcards.front: Why must you never measure distance on the longitude scale?
flashcards.back: Meridians converge towards the poles, so the spacing varies with latitude and gives incorrect distances

flashcards.front: What do the letters (T), (M), and (C) mean after a bearing?
flashcards.back: (T) = True, (M) = Magnetic, (C) = Compass

flashcards.front: When recording a position, which is written first — latitude or longitude?
flashcards.back: Latitude first, then longitude

flashcards.front: What is a relative bearing?
flashcards.back: A bearing measured from the vessel's bow (not from north)

mcqQuestions.question: Q: What is the unit of speed used at sea?
One nautical mile is equal to how many minutes of latitude?
When measuring distance on a chart, which scale must be used?
options: The longitude scale at the top or bottom of the chart
The latitude scale on the sides of the chart
A separate distance bar scale only

mcqQuestions.question: A bearing of 246°(T) means:
A vessel is travelling at 6 knots. How far will it travel in 30 minutes?

title: Navigational Charts and Publications
description: Navigational charts are the fundamental tool of coastal navigation.
summary: Navigational charts are the fundamental tool of coastal navigation. British Admiralty (UKHO) charts use standardised symbols and abbreviations defined in NP5011 (Chart 5011).

keyTerms.definition: The datum to which all charted depths and drying heights are referred; approximates to Lowest Astronomical Tide (LAT)
A depth measurement shown on the chart in metres and decimetres, measured below Chart Datum
The height above Chart Datum of a feature that covers at high water and uncovers at low water; shown as an underlined figure
A rock whose highest point is at or very near Chart Datum (always at the waterline at lowest tide); shown with K12 symbol
A sunken or stranded vessel; dangerous wrecks are enclosed in a dotted danger line on the chart
A line created by two charted marks in transit, indicating a safe track into harbour or through a channel
Two charted objects observed in line from the vessel; provides a precise position line or track
The mathematical model of the earth's shape on which a chart's coordinate system is based; must match GPS settings
A line on a synoptic chart connecting points of equal atmospheric pressure (not a chart symbol per se, but appears on meteorological charts)
Notice to Mariners — official publication of chart corrections issued by the UKHO

safetyNotes: A wreck symbol enclosed in a dotted (danger) line means the wreck is a hazard to surface navigation. Do not attempt to pass over without confirmed, current depth information.
Rocks marked K13 (submerged, depth unknown) must be treated as potentially dangerous regardless of the charted depth in the vicinity. Always give such symbols a generous clearance.
Depth qualifiers ED (Existence Doubtful), SD (Sounding Doubtful), Rep (Reported), and PA (Position Approximate) indicate reduced survey reliability — treat these areas with extra caution.
The geodetic datum on the chart must match the GPS datum setting. A mismatch can displace a plotted position by tens or hundreds of metres — enough to place a vessel on a charted hazard.

flashcards.front: What does an underlined sounding figure on a chart indicate?
flashcards.back: A drying height — the feature is above Chart Datum and dries at low water; the figure is the height above CD in metres.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between the K12 and K13 rock symbols?
flashcards.back: K12 (plus in circle with dots) = rock awash at Chart Datum — always at or very near the surface. K13 (plus with dots, no circle) = submerged rock, depth unknown — always underwater but potentially shallow.

flashcards.front: What format is used for a light description on an Admiralty chart?
flashcards.back: Characteristic, Colour, Period, Elevation, Range — e.g., Fl(3) R 10s 13m 5M.

flashcards.front: Why must you never use the longitude scale to measure distance on a chart?
flashcards.back: Because lines of longitude converge toward the poles — the spacing in millimetres varies with latitude. Only the latitude scale (1' = 1 nautical mile) gives consistent distance measurement.

flashcards.front: What does a magenta diamond with a letter (◇A) on a chart represent?
flashcards.back: A tidal diamond — it refers to a table in the chart's title block giving tidal stream direction and rate for each hour relative to HW at a standard port.

flashcards.front: How are chart corrections promulgated and recorded?
flashcards.back: Through Notices to Mariners (NtM) issued by the UKHO; correction dates are recorded in the chart's title block. Block corrections are applied when a new edition or new print is issued.

mcqQuestions.question: Q: On a metric Admiralty chart, a sounding reads 4₂. What depth does this represent?
Which colour indicates a drying area (covers and uncovers with tide) on a standard British Admiralty chart?
A chart symbol shows a plus sign (+) enclosed in a circle with a dotted surround. What does this indicate?
options: A submerged rock of unknown depth
A rock awash at Chart Datum

mcqQuestions.question: What is the correct scale to use when measuring distance on a chart?
options: The longitude scale on the top or bottom margin
The latitude scale on the side margin at the same latitude as your position
Any scale on the chart title block

mcqQuestions.question: A light is described as Fl(3) R 10s 13m 5M. What does "13m" refer to?
options: The depth of water below the lighthouse
The height of the light above Chart Datum
The height of the light above Mean High Water Springs (MHWS)

mcqQuestions.question: What publication contains the full key to symbols and abbreviations used on Admiralty charts?

title: Navigational Drawing Instruments
description: This topic covers the instruments used at the chart table for plotting positions, transferring bearings, and measuring distance on a nautical chart.
summary: This topic covers the instruments used at the chart table for plotting positions, transferring bearings, and measuring distance on a nautical chart. The principal tools are parallel rulers (walking or rolling), dividers, and proprietary plotters (such as the Portland or Breton plotter).

keyTerms.definition: Linked straight edges that remain parallel while walking across a chart; used to transfer bearings
Parallel rulers operating on a rolling cylinder; same function as linked rulers
Transparent protractor-grid plotter that eliminates the need for a compass rose
Alternative name for a Portland-style plotter
Two-legged instrument used to measure distances on a chart
Printed circle on a chart showing true and magnetic north, used to read off bearings
The vertical scale on the sides of a chart; used to measure distance
Moving parallel rulers across the chart step by step while maintaining alignment

flashcards.front: What are parallel rulers used for in chartwork?
flashcards.back: Transferring a bearing from the compass rose to a point on the chart, or from a feature to the compass rose

flashcards.front: Why must you never use the longitude scale to measure distance?
flashcards.back: Meridians converge towards the poles, making longitude spacing vary with latitude and giving incorrect distances

flashcards.front: What advantage does a Portland/Breton plotter have over parallel rulers?
flashcards.back: It does not require access to the compass rose — the protractor grid is built in

flashcards.front: What is always used for chartwork — pencil or pen?
flashcards.back: Pencil — so workings can be erased

flashcards.front: When using dividers to measure distance, at which level should you read the latitude scale?
flashcards.back: At the same latitude as your position

mcqQuestions.question: Q: What is the primary purpose of dividers in chart navigation?
options: Transferring bearings from the compass rose

mcqQuestions.question: When using parallel rulers to transfer a bearing, what is the main practical risk?
options: The rulers slipping and losing alignment while walking across the chart
Setting the wrong bearing on the compass rose
Drawing the line in the wrong direction

mcqQuestions.question: A plotter is set to 246°(T) and aligned with a chart feature. What must be done next before drawing the bearing line?
options: Transfer the plotter to the compass rose
Line up the plotter's grid with the chart's latitude and longitude lines
Set the dividers to the bearing distance
Walk the plotter to the nearest depth contour

mcqQuestions.question: Which scale on a nautical chart must be used to measure distance with dividers?
options: The latitude scale on the sides of the chart
The distance bar scale at the chart's margin

title: Compass
description: The compass is the primary instrument for steering and taking bearings at sea.
summary: The compass is the primary instrument for steering and taking bearings at sea. Navigators must understand three north references — True North (geographic), Magnetic North (compass rose), and Compass North (the ship's compass reading) — and be able to convert between them.

keyTerms.definition: The direction of the geographic North Pole; the reference for chart directions
The direction toward the Earth's magnetic north pole; the reference to which a compass needle points
The direction indicated by the ship's compass on a given heading, after the effects of onboard magnetic influences (deviation)
The angular difference between True North and Magnetic North at a given position; expressed as East or West
The angular difference between Magnetic North and Compass North on a given vessel heading, caused by onboard magnetic influences
The combined total of variation and deviation; the total angular difference between True North and the compass reading
A table or curve listing the deviation of the ship's compass for each heading, produced by swinging the compass

safetyNotes: Applying variation or deviation in the wrong direction (adding instead of subtracting, or vice versa) produces a compass course error of twice the variation/deviation value. A 7°W variation applied the wrong way results in a 14° heading error — enough to steer into a hazard over a short passage. Always use the conversion diagram (True → +W/−E → Magnetic → +W/−E → Compass) methodically.

Deviation changes with the vessel's heading. A deviation card produced on a previous heading is not valid when the boat has altered course significantly. Always use the deviation card value for the current heading.

Objects that cause deviation (mobile phones, metal mugs, speakers, electronics) must be kept well clear of the ship's compass and especially the hand-bearing compass when taking bearings. A mobile phone held near a hand-bearing compass will produce an erroneous bearing and a false position line.

If compass error is unknown or has not been checked recently, all compass courses and bearings must be treated with additional caution. Swinging the compass and producing a current deviation card is a fundamental pre-passage safety check.

flashcards.front: What is variation?
flashcards.back: The angular difference between True North and Magnetic North at a given position. It is printed on the chart compass rose and changes slowly over time.

flashcards.front: What is deviation?
flashcards.back: The angular difference between Magnetic North and Compass North, caused by ferrous metals and electromagnetic fields onboard. It varies with the vessel's heading.

flashcards.front: If variation is 5°W and you have a true bearing of 080°(T), what is the magnetic bearing?
flashcards.back: 085°(M). West variation means magnetic is greater — add 5°.

flashcards.front: How do you convert a True bearing to a Compass course?
flashcards.back: Apply variation: True + W variation (or − E) = Magnetic. Then apply deviation: Magnetic + W deviation (or − E) = Compass.

flashcards.front: Where do you find the value of variation for your position?
flashcards.back: On the compass rose of the relevant chart. It also shows the annual rate of change so you can apply an update for the current year.

mcqQuestions.question: Q: A chart shows a compass rose with variation 7°W. You have plotted a True course of 055°(T). What is the equivalent Magnetic course?
What causes deviation in a ship's compass?
options: The difference between True North and Magnetic North
Onboard ferrous metals and electromagnetic fields
The annual change in the Earth's magnetic field

mcqQuestions.question: The ship's compass reads 070°(C). The transit you are pointed at bears 068°(M). What is the deviation on this heading?
Chartwork is carried out in which reference system?

title: Chartwork
description: Chartwork is the practical application of navigation on the chart.
summary: Chartwork is the practical application of navigation on the chart. It encompasses plotting positions (by latitude/longitude, by bearing and distance, or by GPS), tracking the vessel's progress using dead reckoning and estimated position, fixing position visually using cross bearings and transits, and planning a course to steer that accounts for tidal stream and leeway.

keyTerms.definition: An approximate position calculated from course steered and distance run, without correction for tide or leeway
A position derived from a DR position by additionally applying leeway (water track) and tidal set and drift
A position determined by external observations (bearings, GPS, transit) rather than calculation
The actual direction of travel of the vessel across the seabed, accounting for tide and leeway
The actual speed of the vessel over the seabed, accounting for tidal stream
The compass course to steer (water track) that, when combined with the tidal stream, results in the desired ground track
The angular difference between the course steered (direction the bow points) and the water track (direction through the water), caused by wind
The direction in which the tidal stream flows (in degrees True)
The distance a vessel is carried by the tidal stream in a given time (or the rate in knots)
The direction and distance the vessel moves through the water, after allowing for leeway but before applying tidal stream
The actual path of the vessel over the seabed, after the combined effects of course steered, leeway, and tidal stream
A position stored in the GPS memory for navigation reference
The small triangle formed when three position lines from a visual fix do not meet at a single point

safetyNotes: A DR position assumes no tidal set or drift — in areas with strong tidal streams it can diverge rapidly from the vessel's actual position. Always progress to an EP (applying leeway and tidal vector) and confirm with a visual fix or GPS at regular intervals. Never rely on a DR position alone in pilotage waters or near hazards.

When three bearing lines form a cocked hat, always assume your position is at the corner of the cocked hat closest to the nearest danger. Do not place yourself at the centre of the cocked hat. This conservative approach is a fundamental safety rule.

GPS gives Course Over Ground (COG) and Speed Over Ground (SOG) relative to the seabed, but the bearing it shows to a waypoint does not account for tidal set. If you steer the GPS waypoint bearing in a cross-tide, you will be set sideways — potentially onto a hazard. Always pre-calculate a course to steer that incorporates the tidal vector.

GPS can fail without warning. Power or aerial failure, mobile phone interference, or changes to the satellite system can all produce a loss of fix or an incorrect position with no alarm. Maintain an independent position record on the paper chart throughout every passage.

Waypoints must always be plotted on the chart and checked before entry into the GPS. A single transposed digit in latitude or longitude can place a waypoint miles from the intended position. The GPS distance and bearing to the waypoint must be cross-checked against the chart-measured values before proceeding.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a DR position and an EP?
flashcards.back: A DR position uses only course steered and distance run. An EP additionally applies leeway (to get water track) and the tidal vector — making it a better approximation of actual position.

flashcards.front: What chartwork symbol is used for (a) a DR position, (b) an EP, and (c) a fix?
flashcards.back: (a) Triangle △; (b) half-triangle (open triangle) ◁; (c) circle ○.

flashcards.front: How do you construct a Course to Steer graphically on the chart?
flashcards.back: Plot ground track from A to B. From A, plot tidal vector. From the end of the tidal vector, swing an arc equal to boat speed to cut the ground track. The bearing of the line from tidal vector end to the cutting point is the water track = CTS.

flashcards.front: What is a cocked hat and where do you place your position within it?
flashcards.back: The small triangle formed when three bearing lines in a visual fix do not intersect at a single point. Place your estimated position at the corner of the cocked hat closest to any nearby hazard.

flashcards.front: Why must GPS be backed up with other position information?
flashcards.back: GPS can fail (power/aerial failure, interference, satellite issues) and gives no warning when it provides an incorrect position. An independent backup prevents navigational error.

flashcards.front: What is the danger of steering the bearing that a GPS gives you directly to a waypoint when there is cross-tide?
flashcards.back: The tidal stream will push you off the straight-line track — you will sail a longer route and may be set toward a hazard. Always calculate a course to steer that accounts for the tide.

mcqQuestions.question: Q: A vessel steers 070°(T) for 1 hour at 5 knots and the log records 5 miles. The tidal stream is 120°(T) at 1.5 knots. What is the vessel's approximate position?
options: The resultant of 5 miles on 070°(T) plus 1.5 miles on 120°(T)

mcqQuestions.question: What does the symbol ◁ (half-triangle) represent on a chart?
Three bearing lines on a chart form a small triangle. Where should you assume your position to be?
options: At the centroid (centre) of the triangle
At the corner of the triangle closest to the nearest hazard
At the corner of the triangle farthest from land
At the point where two of the three lines cross

mcqQuestions.question: When using a transit as a position line, what is the ideal angle for the second bearing to intersect the transit?
You input a waypoint into your GPS from the chart. The GPS shows BRG 010° DIST 6.7M. You plot the waypoint on the chart and measure the bearing as 012° at 6.9M. What does this discrepancy suggest?
options: You probably entered the wrong latitude or longitude
Tidal stream is affecting the display

title: Tides and Tidal Streams
description: Tides are the regular rise and fall of sea level caused primarily by the gravitational pull of the Moon and Sun.
summary: Tides are the regular rise and fall of sea level caused primarily by the gravitational pull of the Moon and Sun. Understanding tidal heights is essential for calculating the depth of water over shoals, under bridges, and at harbour entrances.

keyTerms.definition: The datum below which depths are charted; approximates to Lowest Astronomical Tide
Lowest Astronomical Tide — the lowest predicted tidal level under average meteorological conditions
Highest Astronomical Tide — the highest predicted tidal level under average meteorological conditions
Mean High Water Springs — the average height of all spring high waters
Mean High Water Neaps — the average height of all neap high waters
Mean Low Water Neaps — the average height of all neap low waters
Mean Low Water Springs — the average height of all spring low waters
High Water — the highest level reached during a tidal cycle
Low Water — the lowest level reached during a tidal cycle
The difference between HW height and LW height for a given tidal cycle
A tide with a greater-than-average range, occurring near new and full moon
A tide with a smaller-than-average range, occurring near first and third quarter moon
A magenta diamond symbol (◇ + letter) on the chart referring to a tidal stream data table in the chart's title block
The direction in which the tidal stream is flowing (°True)
The speed of the tidal stream in knots
The vertical distance of the water surface above Chart Datum at any given time

safetyNotes: Tidal height calculations directly determine whether there is sufficient water under the keel. An error of even 0.5 m can result in grounding on a shallow bar, a drying rock, or in the approach to a drying harbour. Always verify calculations and allow an adequate safety margin.

Heights of lights on charts are above MHWS — not Chart Datum. Do not use light elevation as a guide to clearance under bridges or overhead cables; refer to the charted clearance height (which is above MHWS or HAT, as specified).

When calculating secondary port heights, interpolation between tabulated differences is required when the standard port HW time falls between reference times. Failure to interpolate correctly will produce an incorrect height of tide.

Tidal stream strength affects all aspects of passage planning: failing to allow for a foul stream can leave a vessel short of a destination at nightfall or in deteriorating weather, or can set it onto a lee shore or into a hazardous area.

flashcards.front: What is the rule of twelfths?
flashcards.back: The tide rises or falls approximately 1/12, 2/12, 3/12, 3/12, 2/12, 1/12 of the total range in each successive hour from LW to HW. The tide moves fastest in the middle two hours.

flashcards.front: How do you calculate the depth of water over a charted shoal at a given time?
flashcards.back: Depth of water = charted depth + height of tide. (For a drying feature: depth of water = height of tide − drying height.)

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a standard port and a secondary port?
flashcards.back: A standard port has full daily tide tables. A secondary port does not — you apply time and height differences (from an almanac) to the nearest standard port to find its tidal information.

flashcards.front: How do you determine whether conditions are springs or neaps?
flashcards.back: Calculate the range (HW height − LW height) and compare with the mean spring range and mean neap range printed on the tidal curve for that port.

flashcards.front: What information does a tidal diamond table give you?
flashcards.back: For each hour before and after HW at a reference port: the direction (°T) and rate (knots) of the tidal stream at that position, for both spring and neap conditions.

flashcards.front: What is the reference datum for light elevation on a chart, and how does it differ from Chart Datum?
flashcards.back: Light elevation is given above MHWS (Mean High Water Springs). Chart Datum is approximately LAT — well below MHWS. They are different references and must not be confused.

mcqQuestions.question: Q: At a given time the height of tide is 3.2 m. The chart shows a drying height of 1.4 m for a rock. Is the rock covered or uncovered, and by how much water?
options: Uncovered — the rock dries 1.4 m above the surface

mcqQuestions.question: When should you use the solid curve (rather than the dotted curve) on a tidal curve diagram?
options: When the range is less than the mean range
At all times unless the port is a secondary port

mcqQuestions.question: A tidal stream arrow on the chart shows the figures 14,28. What do these numbers represent?
options: Spring rate 1.4 kn, neap rate 2.8 kn
Spring rate 14 kn, neap rate 28 kn

mcqQuestions.question: You need to find the height of tide at 0820 at Plymouth. Which instrument / publication would you use?
options: The compass rose on the chart
The Plymouth tidal curve, using the day's HW and LW heights from the tide tables
The tidal diamond table on the chart

mcqQuestions.question: HW at a standard port is 5.6 m, LW is 0.8 m. The mean spring range for that port is 4.8 m and mean neap range is 2.4 m. Is today's tide closer to springs or neaps?
options: Neaps — the range of 4.8 m equals the mean spring range exactly
Springs — the range of 4.8 m matches the mean spring range
Neaps — the range of 4.8 m is midway between the two
Cannot be determined without checking the moon phase

title: Visual Aids to Navigation — Lighthouse and Beacon Light Characteristics, IALA Buoyage System
description: The IALA Maritime Buoyage System divides the world into two regions.
summary: The IALA Maritime Buoyage System divides the world into two regions. Region A covers Europe, Africa, Russia, India, Australia, and New Zealand; Region B covers the Americas, Japan, South Korea, the Philippines, and Canada.

keyTerms.definition: Buoyage region covering Europe, Africa, Russia, India, Australia, New Zealand — red to port
Buoyage region covering the Americas, Japan, South Korea, Philippines — green to port (reversed)
A buoy or beacon marking the sides of a navigable channel; colour and shape depend on IALA region
A black-and-yellow mark indicating the safe side to pass relative to a danger; four types: N, S, E, W
A black-and-red mark placed over a danger surrounded by navigable water; topmark two black spheres
A red-and-white vertically striped mark indicating navigable water all around; used as fairway/landfall buoy
A yellow mark indicating a special area or feature not used for general navigation
A blue-and-yellow vertically striped mark deployed over a newly discovered uncharted wreck
A junction mark with Fl(2+1) light indicating the principal (preferred) channel at a bifurcation
The time in seconds for one complete cycle of a light characteristic, e.g. "Fl G 5s" has a 5-second period
A light exhibiting different colours in different arcs; used to define safe/dangerous approach sectors
Two lights arranged so vertical alignment places the vessel on the correct leading line into harbour
A distinctive shape fixed to the top of a buoy or beacon to aid daytime identification
The conventional direction in which lateral marks are assigned: generally from seaward into harbour
Light rhythm of 50–79 flashes per minute; used on cardinal marks
Light rhythm of 80–159 flashes per minute; used on cardinal marks

safetyNotes: Region B reversal — critical hazard: In IALA Region B (Americas, Japan, South Korea, Philippines), the lateral mark colours are reversed relative to Region A. A navigator trained in Region A who treats red marks as port-hand and green as starboard-hand in Region B waters will be navigating on the wrong side of every channel. Always confirm the applicable IALA region before entering unfamiliar waters.

Cardinal mark confusion — South vs West: Both South and West cardinals show yellow/black banding. The distinguishing feature at night is the long flash (LFl) appended to the South cardinal's six quick flashes. Confusing the two could lead a vessel toward danger rather than away from it.

Isolated danger mark — do not approach: The isolated danger mark is placed directly over the danger. "Navigable water all around" means the surrounding water is clear, not that the mark itself is safe to approach. Pass well clear.

Topmarks degraded by weather: Weed, guano, and solar panels can obscure or alter the appearance of topmarks in real conditions. Do not rely solely on topmark identification; cross-check with light characteristics and chart.

Emergency wreck marking buoy — not on chart: By definition, an emergency wreck marking buoy marks a danger that is not yet charted. If one is encountered, the wreck beneath or nearby is uncharted and the immediate area should be avoided.

Fl(2+1) reserved for preferred channel marks: The composite group flashing rhythm is used exclusively for junction marks. Any other mark showing Fl(2+1) would be anomalous; investigate before proceeding.

flashcards.front: What colour, shape, and light does a port-hand lateral mark show in IALA Region A?
flashcards.back: Red, can/pillar/spar, any red rhythm except Fl(2+1)

flashcards.front: What colour, shape, and light does a starboard-hand lateral mark show in IALA Region A?
flashcards.back: Green, conical/pillar/spar, any green rhythm except Fl(2+1)

flashcards.front: What is the light characteristic unique to preferred channel (junction) marks?
flashcards.back: Fl(2+1) — composite group flashing (two flashes, pause, one flash)

flashcards.front: How do you identify a North cardinal mark by day and by night?
flashcards.back: Day — black over yellow body, two cones pointing up; Night — Q or VQ continuous quick white

flashcards.front: What is the light characteristic of a South cardinal mark, and how does it differ from a West cardinal?
flashcards.back: South = Q(6)+LFl or VQ(6)+LFl — the long flash distinguishes it from West (Q(9) or VQ(9), no long flash)

flashcards.front: What topmark is mandatory on an isolated danger mark, and what is its light?
flashcards.back: Two black spheres; Fl(2) white

flashcards.front: What is the only mark that uses Mo(A) or LFl 10s as a light characteristic?
flashcards.back: Safe water mark (red and white vertical stripes)

flashcards.front: What colour and light does a special mark show?
flashcards.back: Yellow body; yellow light, any rhythm (often Fl Y)

flashcards.front: What is the key visual difference between an emergency wreck marking buoy and all other marks?
flashcards.back: Blue and yellow vertical stripes plus unique upright yellow cross topmark

flashcards.front: When two leading lights are vertically aligned, what does this indicate?
flashcards.back: The vessel is on the correct leading line (on track)

mcqQuestions.question: Q: In IALA Region A, what colour is the port-hand lateral mark?
A buoy has black and yellow horizontal bands, two cones pointing downward, and shows Q(6)+LFl white. What type of mark is it?
What is the unique light characteristic of a preferred channel (junction) mark?
A vessel approaching a harbour in IALA Region B should treat lateral mark colours as:
options: Identical to Region A — red to port, green to starboard
Reversed from Region A — green to port, red to starboard

mcqQuestions.question: What is the topmark of an isolated danger mark?
options: Two black spheres, one above the other

mcqQuestions.question: A safe water mark can be identified by its:
options: Red and white vertical stripes with a red spherical topmark

mcqQuestions.question: An emergency wreck marking buoy is deployed to mark:
options: A newly discovered uncharted wreck awaiting permanent marking
The boundary of a military exercise area

mcqQuestions.question: A sector light shows white to vessels in the safe sector and red in the danger sector. The characteristic is Iso.WR.4s 20m 10M/7M. What does "Iso" tell you?
options: The light is fixed and continuous
The light flashes rapidly (50+ times per minute)
The periods of light and darkness are equal
The light occults (darkness shorter than light)

title: Meteorology
description: Understanding weather is fundamental to safe passage planning.
summary: Understanding weather is fundamental to safe passage planning. Day Skipper students must be able to obtain and interpret marine weather forecasts (including the Shipping Forecast), understand pressure systems, fronts, and their associated weather, interpret the Beaufort wind scale, recognise land and sea breeze effects, and understand the significance of deteriorating weather for a vessel underway.

keyTerms.definition: A line on a synoptic chart connecting points of equal atmospheric pressure
The boundary between two air masses of different temperature and humidity
The leading edge of a warm air mass advancing over colder air; shown as red line with semicircles
The leading edge of a cold air mass advancing under warm air; shown as blue line with triangles; brings squally weather
A front formed when a cold front catches and lifts a warm front off the surface
An elongated area of lower pressure extending from a depression; often associated with a line of cloud, showers, or squally winds
An elongated area of higher pressure between two depressions; typically brings a brief improvement
A standardised scale (0–12) describing wind speed and sea state
A weather map showing isobars, fronts, and pressure systems at a given time
Wind changing direction anticlockwise (e.g., NW to SW)
Wind changing direction clockwise (e.g., NE to SE)
A condition where wind direction opposes the tidal stream direction, creating short steep dangerous waves

safetyNotes: Wind over tide (opposing wind and tidal stream) creates short, steep, breaking waves significantly more dangerous than the wind force alone would suggest. When wind is against a strong spring tide, even Force 4–5 can produce hazardous sea states. Plan passages to avoid wind-over-tide at key headlands or tidal gates.

At the first sign of deteriorating weather: obtain an updated forecast; consider altering course for a safe haven; inform crew; brief them on safety procedures; reduce sail early. It is always easier to make decisions early than in worsening conditions.

A gale warning (Force 6+) issued for a sea area means conditions are likely to be unsuitable for small craft. Day Skipper students should not put to sea when a gale warning is in force for their intended area.

flashcards.front: In the Northern Hemisphere, which direction does wind circulate around a low pressure system, and which around a high?
flashcards.back: Low (depression): anticlockwise. High (anticyclone): clockwise.

flashcards.front: What are the Beaufort Force 5 characteristics?
flashcards.back: Fresh breeze, 17–21 knots; moderate waves with many white crests; sailing vessels should reef the mainsail.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between "backing" and "veering"?
flashcards.back: Backing = wind changing anticlockwise (e.g., NW → SW). Veering = wind changing clockwise (e.g., NE → SE).

flashcards.front: What is the visibility category "Poor" in a shipping forecast?
flashcards.back: 1,000 m to 2 nautical miles. (Fog = less than 1,000 m.)

flashcards.front: What is "wind over tide" and why is it dangerous?
flashcards.back: When the wind opposes the tidal stream, creating short, steep waves out of proportion to the wind strength — more hazardous than equivalent wind-with-tide conditions.

flashcards.front: What triggers a gale warning?
flashcards.back: Average wind Force 6 or above, or gusts of 43–51 knots. Force 6 is sometimes called a "yachtsman's gale".

mcqQuestions.question: Q: In the Northern Hemisphere, a depression is approaching from the west. As the warm front passes over your position, what typically happens to the wind direction?
A weather forecast describes conditions as "Imminent." How soon is the forecast event expected?
options: More than 12 hours from the time of issue

mcqQuestions.question: Which Beaufort Force is associated with "large waves, white foam crests" and is the threshold usually described as "Strong breeze"?
You are planning to enter a bay in the afternoon. The forecast shows a light northerly offshore wind, sunshine, and no frontal activity. What local weather effect should you anticipate?
options: Land breeze — cool air flowing out to sea
Sea breeze — warm air rising over land, onshore flow up to Force 4
A trough bringing rain and backing winds
No local effect — the synoptic pattern dominates

mcqQuestions.question: Isobars are closely spaced on a synoptic chart. What does this indicate?
options: A large high pressure system bringing settled weather

title: Passage Planning
description: Passage planning is the process of preparing systematically for a voyage — from initial appraisal through to arrival.
summary: Passage planning is the process of preparing systematically for a voyage — from initial appraisal through to arrival. Good passage planning considers the navigational environment, weather, tidal windows, hazards, and contingency options.

keyTerms.definition: A position stored in the GPS, used to define the planned track
The planned line on the chart from departure to destination (or between waypoints)
Cross-Track Error — the GPS-derived perpendicular distance the vessel is from the planned track
Estimated Time of Arrival at a destination or waypoint
Estimated Time of Departure
Estimated Time En route
A rota system for dividing the crew into groups that take turns on deck, ensuring the vessel is always manned while some crew rest
The navigational record kept underway; records position, course, speed, log reading, weather, and significant events
A bearing that, when maintained (e.g., "not less than 043°M"), keeps the vessel clear of a specific hazard
Two charted objects observed in line from the vessel; provides an accurate position line or turning point
Inshore navigation using visual references (transits, buoys, clearing bearings, depth contours)

safetyNotes: A proper look-out must be maintained at all times underway. COLREGS Rule 5: "Every vessel shall at all times maintain a proper look-out by sight and hearing as well as by all available means appropriate in the prevailing circumstances and conditions so as to make a full appraisal of the situation and of the risk of collision." [colregs-irpcs-text.md, Rule 5]
Never place a waypoint directly on a hazard — always offset it to pass safely. Steering direct to a waypoint without prior cross-tide calculation can set the vessel toward the hazard.
Always obtain an up-to-date weather forecast before departure and at regular intervals during a passage. Be prepared to divert to a safe haven if conditions deteriorate.
Wind over tide at harbour entrances, bars, and tidal gates can create steep, dangerous seas even in moderate wind strengths. Plan passage timing to avoid these conditions.

flashcards.front: What are the six pilotage techniques described in the RYA Day Skipper material?
flashcards.back: (1) Transits; (2) following depth contours; (3) back bearings; (4) clearing bearings; (5) bearing and distance from a mark; (6) turning points defined by a bearing.

flashcards.front: What is a clearing bearing and how is it used?
flashcards.back: A bearing that, when maintained (e.g., "keep not less than 043°M"), ensures the vessel remains clear of a specified hazard. Two clearing bearings can define a safe corridor between two hazards.

flashcards.front: What should you check immediately after entering a waypoint into a GPS?
flashcards.back: Compare the bearing and distance shown by the GPS with the bearing and distance you have measured on the chart. Any discrepancy means the waypoint was probably entered incorrectly.

flashcards.front: Why should bearings in a pilotage plan be written in Magnetic or Compass rather than True?
flashcards.back: Because the skipper and helmsman use these bearings directly on deck with the ship's compass or hand-bearing compass, which work in Magnetic or Compass — converting True at sea under pressure leads to errors.

flashcards.front: Name four things a Day Skipper should plan for when making a pilotage plan.
flashcards.back: Any four from: rocks exposed at different states of tide; shoals and shallows; shipping channels; chain ferries; tidal effect on track; harbour bylaws; speed restrictions in channels; small craft channels; change of buoyage system.

flashcards.front: What does XTE stand for, and what does it tell you?
flashcards.back: Cross-Track Error — the perpendicular distance from the vessel's GPS position to the planned track. A large XTE means the vessel has been pushed off track, e.g., by tide or leeway.

mcqQuestions.question: Q: Which of the following is NOT a recognised pilotage technique?
options: Calculating leeway from the Beaufort scale

mcqQuestions.question: A pilotage plan note reads "Turn when chimney bears 320°(T)". What type of technique is this?
options: Turning point defined by a bearing on a charted object

mcqQuestions.question: You enter a harbour waypoint into your GPS from the chart. The GPS shows bearing 205° and distance 4.2M. On the chart you measure the bearing as 195° and distance 4.2M. What is the most likely cause?
options: The tidal stream is affecting the GPS display
You have entered the wrong longitude or latitude
Magnetic variation is not applied in the GPS
The chart datum does not match the GPS datum

mcqQuestions.question: What does a clearing bearing of "not less than 043°(M)" mean in practice?
options: The vessel must steer 043°(M) to be safe
As long as the bearing to the charted mark reads 043°M or more, the vessel is clear of the hazard
The vessel must never steer less than 043°M
043°(M) is the true bearing of the safe track

mcqQuestions.question: When planning a coastal passage, which of the following best describes when to plan for a fair tidal stream?
options: At the start of the passage only
Particularly at tidal gates, harbour bars, and headlands where the stream is strongest
Only when the passage exceeds 20 miles

title: Navigation in Restricted Visibility
description: Restricted visibility occurs whenever visibility is reduced by fog, mist, falling snow, heavy rain, sandstorms, or any other similar cause.
summary: Restricted visibility occurs whenever visibility is reduced by fog, mist, falling snow, heavy rain, sandstorms, or any other similar cause. It is one of the most hazardous conditions a skipper can encounter because: - Other vessels may not be seen until dangerously close.

keyTerms.definition: Any condition in which visibility is reduced by fog, mist, falling snow, heavy rain, sandstorms, or any other similar cause.
A sound signal made at regular prescribed intervals by a vessel in or near an area of restricted visibility, to warn other vessels of its presence.
A speed at which the vessel can take proper and effective action to avoid collision and stop within the distance appropriate to the prevailing circumstances and visibility.
The lowest speed at which a vessel retains directional control. In restricted visibility, a vessel hearing a fog signal forward of the beam must reduce to this speed.
Radio Detection And Ranging — an electronic instrument that detects targets by sending and receiving reflected radio pulses. Essential for collision avoidance in restricted visibility.
Automatic Radar Plotting Aid — a radar function that automatically tracks targets and calculates their course, speed, and closest point of approach (CPA).
Closest Point of Approach — the nearest distance a target vessel will reach relative to own vessel if both maintain current courses and speeds. Used in radar collision avoidance.
The vessel's best estimate of its position based on dead reckoning from the last known fix, accounting for course, speed, and tidal stream. More uncertain than a fix; critical to maintain in fog.

safetyNotes: In fog, do NOT increase speed to "get clear quickly" — this is one of the most common and dangerous errors. Speed must be REDUCED.
Never assume that because you cannot see another vessel, it cannot see or hit you — a large ship may appear within seconds of its fog signal being audible.
Sound signals in fog are mandatory. Failing to make fog signals, or making them incorrectly, can contribute directly to a collision.
A radar is only as useful as the person interpreting it — a beginner should seek additional training in radar collision avoidance (ARPA and plotting).
Do NOT alter course to port for a vessel detected forward of the beam in restricted visibility (Rule 19 prohibition).
If in any doubt about safety in fog, the prudent action is to anchor clear of shipping lanes, make the correct sound signals, and wait for conditions to improve.

flashcards.front: What does IRPCS Rule 19 govern?
flashcards.back: The conduct of vessels in or near areas of restricted visibility. It applies when vessels are not in sight of one another.

flashcards.front: What is the first action a skipper should take when entering fog?
flashcards.back: Reduce speed, post a dedicated forward look-out, switch on navigation lights, begin making fog signals, brief crew, prepare emergency equipment, and establish the vessel's position.

flashcards.front: If you hear a fog signal forward of the beam, what should you do?
flashcards.back: Reduce speed to minimum steerage way and navigate with extreme caution until the risk of collision is past.

flashcards.front: What day shape does a vessel display when at anchor?
flashcards.back: A black ball (one ball, forward, where it can best be seen).

flashcards.front: Why should you NOT rely solely on visual watch in fog?
flashcards.back: Visibility may be insufficient to see another vessel until it is too close to avoid; radar, sound, and radio monitoring must supplement visual watch.

flashcards.front: What is the fog sound signal for a power-driven vessel making way?
flashcards.back: One prolonged blast at intervals of not more than 2 minutes.

mcqQuestions.question: Under IRPCS Rule 19, when you detect another vessel on radar forward of the beam in restricted visibility, what action is prohibited?
When must navigation lights be switched on?
options: Only when in a shipping lane
In restricted visibility at any time of day, as well as between sunset and sunrise

mcqQuestions.question: Which of the following best describes "safe speed" in restricted visibility?
options: The vessel's maximum speed, to clear the area quickly
A speed at which the vessel can stop within the range of visibility and take effective action to avoid collision
Any speed, provided navigation lights are on

mcqQuestions.question: A sailing vessel of 12 m is motoring in fog. Which combination of lights must she display at night?
options: Tricolour masthead light AND steaming light

title: Pilotage — Transits, Leading Lines, IALA Buoyage, Sailing Directions, Harbour Entry
description: Pilotage is the art of inshore navigation using visual references — marks, transits, depth contours, bearings, and buoys — to navigate safely along the coast and in and out of harbour.
summary: Pilotage is the art of inshore navigation using visual references — marks, transits, depth contours, bearings, and buoys — to navigate safely along the coast and in and out of harbour. Because the navigator is close to hazards, good advance planning is essential: a well-constructed pilotage plan allows the skipper to navigate from the deck without having to go below to work out the next move.

keyTerms.definition: Two charted objects observed in line from a vessel, defining a precise bearing line on the chart
A transit specifically designed to guide a vessel safely through a channel or harbour entrance; often marked by official leading marks or lights
A bearing of a charted object that, when maintained at not less than (or not more than) a stated value, keeps the vessel clear of a specific danger
A written sequential record of marks, bearings, depths, and turning actions for a passage through pilotage waters, prepared in advance
A defined position along a pilotage route at which the navigator expects a specific action (course change, mark identification, bearing check)
A compass bearing taken to a charted object astern, used as a position check or to confirm the vessel remains on track
A line of equal charted depth; can be followed as a track or used to obtain a position line via the echo sounder
The conventional direction in which IALA lateral marks are assigned; generally from seaward toward harbour
The vertical distance of the water surface above chart datum at a given time; added to charted depth to determine actual depth

safetyNotes: Never go below during pilotage: A core principle is deck-based navigation. Going below to consult the chart removes the navigator's situational awareness precisely when it is most needed. All plan elements must be pre-prepared and accessible on deck.

Positive identification before acting: Do not turn or alter course on a buoy until it has been positively identified by colour, shape, topmark, and light characteristic. Acting on the wrong mark in a buoyed channel can lead a vessel onto the hazard the mark was warning about.

Region A / Region B lateral mark reversal: A vessel transiting from European (Region A) to American or Japanese (Region B) waters, or vice versa, must reverse its treatment of all lateral marks. Misidentifying the applicable region puts the vessel on the wrong side of every channel mark.

Tidal clearance is time-critical: A positive clearance calculation applies only at the time it was computed. If the pilotage is delayed, recalculate bar clearance for the revised time of passage — particularly on a falling tide.

Contour following requires continuous echo-sounder watch: Tidal height changes the actual depth continuously. A contour followed at HW may not be followable at LW. Always apply the predicted height of tide to the charted depth before committing to a contour track.

Shipping channels and chain ferries: Vessels in confined waters may be constrained and unable to manoeuvre. A pilotage plan must identify any crossing points with commercial traffic and decide on timing and VHF contact strategy before entering the channel. Rule 9 (Narrow Channels) requires keeping to the starboard side of the channel and not impeding vessels that can only navigate safely within it. [colregs-irpcs-text.md, Rule 9]

flashcards.front: What is a transit, and why is it the most accurate pilotage technique?
flashcards.back: Two charted objects in line; no instrument needed — when objects are aligned the vessel is exactly on the transit bearing

flashcards.front: When using leading lights, what does vertical alignment of the upper and lower lights indicate?
flashcards.back: The vessel is on the correct leading line (on track)

flashcards.front: What is a clearing bearing, and how is it used?
flashcards.back: A bearing of a charted object that, when maintained not less than (or not more than) a given value, keeps the vessel clear of a danger

flashcards.front: List the six elements of a pilotage plan as shown in the RYA training material.
flashcards.back: (1) Transit, (2) Depth contour, (3) Clearance over bar, (4) Positive ID of IALA marks, (5) Back bearing, (6) Clearing lines

flashcards.front: Why should a pilotage plan be written on a notepad rather than kept only on the chart?
flashcards.back: So the skipper can navigate from the deck without going below, keeping a visual watch at all times

flashcards.front: In IALA Region A, a pilotage plan directs you to "leave the green conical buoy to starboard." What does this tell you about your direction of travel?
flashcards.back: You are entering harbour (or proceeding upstream) in Region A

flashcards.front: How is minimum depth clearance over a bar calculated?
flashcards.back: Charted depth plus height of tide at time of passage (or, if given as a net calculation: height of tide minus any safety margin)

mcqQuestions.question: Q: Which of the following best describes a transit in pilotage?
options: A compass bearing taken to a single charted object
Two charted objects observed in line from the vessel's position
A depth contour followed on the echo sounder
A waypoint entered into the chart plotter

mcqQuestions.question: A vessel approaching harbour in IALA Region A should keep green conical buoys to:
options: Either side — colour does not matter

mcqQuestions.question: When the upper leading light appears to the right of the lower leading light, the vessel is:
A clearing bearing of "not less than 043°(M)" on a charted lighthouse means:
options: Keep the lighthouse bearing less than 043°(M)
Keep the lighthouse bearing at or greater than 043°(M) to remain clear of the danger
The lighthouse is 043°(M) from the danger

mcqQuestions.question: Which of the following items should be included in a pilotage plan?
options: Transit bearings and turning points only
Only the compass course to steer
Transits, contour depths, tidal bar clearance, IALA mark identification, back bearings, and clearing bearings

title: Marine Environment
